news_summary: Today's space news highlights NASA's advancements through Artemis II contributions, support for innovative small businesses, intriguing discoveries about new states of matter within Uranus and Neptune, and ongoing Mars exploration progress by Curiosity.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant les avancées de la NASA grâce aux contributions de la mission Artemis II, le soutien apporté aux petites entreprises innovantes, des découvertes fascinantes concernant de nouveaux états de la matière au sein d'Uranus et de Neptune, ainsi que les progrès continus de l'exploration martienne menés par Curiosity.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan los avances de la NASA gracias a las contribuciones de Artemis II, el apoyo a pequeñas empresas innovadoras, los fascinantes descubrimientos sobre nuevos estados de la materia en Urano y Neptuno, y los continuos progresos en la exploración de Marte por parte del Curiosity.

articles.title: What Are Ames’ Contributions to Artemis II?
articles.summary: NASA successfully sent four astronauts around the Moon for the first time in more than 50 years, setting the stage for future lunar landing missions. As the agency continues to push the bounds of space exploration, NASA’s Ames Research Center in California’s Silicon Valley provided essential support in preparing for the mission.  Artemis II was the first crewed test flight under NASA’s […]
articles.content:
NASA Artemis II astronauts Reid Wiseman, commander, left; Christina Koch, mission specialist; CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen, mission specialist; and NASA astronaut Victor Glover, pilot, right, pose for a group photo after viewing the Orion spacecraft in the well deck of the USS John P. Murtha, Saturday, April 11, 2026, in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of California. The quartet splashed down Friday, April 10, at 5:07 p.m. PDT (8:07 p.m. EDT). NASA/Bill Ingalls

NASA successfully sent four astronauts around the Moon for the first time in more than 50 years, setting the stage for future lunar landing missions. As the agency continues to push the bounds of space exploration, NASA’s Ames Research Center in California’s Silicon Valley provided essential support in preparing for the mission.

Artemis II was the first crewed test flight under NASA’s Artemis program. Launching on April 1, 2026, the mission demonstrated systems and hardware needed for deep space missions. Four astronauts – NASA’s Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, and CSA’s (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen – spent approximately 10 days traveling around the Moon and back inside the Orion spacecraft. The test flight built on lessons learned and results from the uncrewed test flight of Artemis I, which launched on November 16, 2022.

Ames continued to build on its contributions from Artemis I, advancing research, engineering, science, and technology for Artemis II.

Orion Spacecraft

After the crew set eyes on the far side of the Moon, making observations that will help us prepare for future lunar exploration, they began a four-day journey home. Orion returned home to Earth on a free return trajectory, being naturally pulled back by Earth’s gravity and entering the atmosphere at about 25,000 mph. Its heat shield protected the spacecraft from temperatures up to 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit during reentry.

NASA learned from Artemis I that Orion’s heat shield experienced more char loss than expected, caused by internal gas buildup during reentry. While Artemis I was uncrewed, flight data showed that had crew been aboard, they would have been safe. Engineers used revised analysis methods and extensive arc jet material testing to help understand root cause, reproduce the char loss, and ensured the heat shield would perform as intended during Orion’s return to Earth on a modified trajectory.

Ames engineers and researchers developed a suite of sensors to provide heat shield performance data during reentry, including temperature and pressure information. Ames also contributed to Orion’s 3D-MAT compression pads, which connect the crew module to the service module. This technology maintains strength under extreme heat while insulating the spacecraft. Developed through collaboration with small businesses, 3D-MAT demonstrates how NASA innovations can impact human spaceflight and beyond.

Understanding the heating conditions Orion faced during reentry as well as potential abort scenarios was key to mission success. The Ames Aerosciences team provided support in these key aerothermal simulations and developed an innovative tool that combines onboard pressure sensor data from Orion with advanced computer modeling. The result predicted the spacecraft’s path back to Earth more accurately, making reentry safer, more precise, and improving mission confidence.

Space Launch System

The SLS rocket experienced higher-than-expected vibrations near the solid rocket booster attach points during Artemis I, caused by unsteady airflow between the boosters and the core stage. To address this, engineers added four strakes – thin, fin-like structures – to the SLS core stage for Artemis II. These strakes change the airflow and reduce vibration, improving safety during ascent. Ames, in collaboration with other centers, played a key role in validating this solution through supercomputer modeling and advanced wind tunnel testing using Unsteady Pressure Sensitive Paint and high-speed cameras.

The team also reviewed potential debris impacts and analyzed the impact of strengthening parts of the vehicle after larger-than-expected debris was observed during Artemis I. Ames engineers also supported launch operations by monitoring aerodynamic data and debris analysis in real time.

This collaboration between wind tunnel engineers, data visualization scientists, and software developers delivered a quick, cost-effective solution that combines physical testing with computational modeling, building on NASA Ames’s history of using supercomputer simulations to further testing and research across the agency. The result is a refined rocket designed and optimized for Artemis II’s historic journey.

Ames funding through the Small Business Innovation Research / Small Business Technology Transfer (SBIR/STTR) program also led to new innovations that supported both Orion and SLS, including advanced material design, software development, safety sensors, and acoustic modeling.

Science

As members of the Artemis II lunar science team, Ames scientists worked with flight operations at NASA’s Mission Control Center at the agency’s Johnson Space Center in Houston to lead and guide the Artemis II crew through the mission’s lunar observations. Key science objectives included studying lunar color, impact history, tectonic features, and future landing sites, as well as characterizing dynamic events such as impact flashes.

The Ames scientists have been members of a team that trained the Artemis II crew over several years to use their eyes – remarkably sensitive instruments – to observe, describe, and interpret geologic variations in lunar features during the flyby. After launch, a timeline of targeted observations built by the lunar science team guided the crew to describe and photograph specific lunar targets, including craters, volcanic formations, and surface colorations. These firsthand observations, paired with imagery from Orion, create a unique dataset to inform future human exploration of the Moon.

Mission Assurance

Ames also supported mission assurance through its Mission and Fault Management team, which helps the agency anticipate and respond to potential problems by testing systems, verifying software, and creating tools to detect issues early through simulation and scenario testing.

The Cross-Program Integrated Data Systems team at Ames developed a suite of software products to support flight readiness, risk assessment, and decision making up to the moment of launch.

During Artemis II, Ames experts served as backup console operators and contributed to real-time analysis, helping NASA respond quickly to unexpected conditions. These efforts strengthened the reliability of critical systems and reduced risk for the crew.

Ames experts are heavily involved in the post-flight data analysis effort assessing the performance of the Mission and Fault Management logic during the Artemis II flight.

Learn more:

Ames contributions to Artemis I: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/artemis/what-are-ames-contributions-to-artemis-i/

For news media:

Artemis II press kit: https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit/

Members of the news media interested in covering this topic should reach out to the NASA Ames newsroom.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on NASA's successful Artemis II mission, the first crewed lunar flyby in over 50 years, highlighting significant technological and scientific contributions from Ames Research Center that improve space exploration safety and knowledge. The mission's broad impact on future lunar exploration and deep space missions is clearly detailed, demonstrating a major advancement in human spaceflight.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Ames Center plays key role in Artemis II lunar mission success
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA recently completed Artemis II, the first mission with astronauts flying around the Moon in over 50 years. This mission tested new technology and prepared for future moon landings. Ames Research Center in California helped make this mission possible.

**What happened** – On April 1, 2026, four astronauts from NASA and the Canadian Space Agency traveled around the Moon in the Orion spacecraft for about 10 days. Ames engineers and scientists contributed by improving the spacecraft’s heat shield, helping reduce rocket vibrations, and developing sensors and software that monitored the mission in real time. They also guided the astronauts in observing the Moon’s surface to gather important scientific data.

**Impact** – Ames’ work made the Artemis II mission safer and more reliable. For example, they fixed problems with the heat shield that protects astronauts during the fiery return to Earth. Their work on rocket airflow reduced dangerous vibrations during launch. The observations made by the crew, directed by Ames scientists, will help choose future landing spots on the Moon. These advances help NASA prepare for longer and more complex space missions.

**What's next step** – The data and technology from Artemis II will support upcoming Artemis missions, including the first human return to the Moon’s surface. Ames will continue testing and improving spacecraft systems, ensuring future missions are even safer and more successful. Scientists will also study the new lunar observations to plan better exploration strategies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Ames Research Center played a crucial role in making Artemis II a safe and successful step toward returning humans to the Moon.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le centre Ames de la NASA joue un rôle clé dans le succès de la mission Artemis II autour de la Lune
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA a récemment réalisé Artemis II, la première mission avec des astronautes autour de la Lune depuis plus de 50 ans. Cette mission a testé de nouvelles technologies et préparé les futurs atterrissages lunaires. Le centre de recherche Ames en Californie a contribué à rendre cette mission possible.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 1er avril 2026, quatre astronautes de la NASA et de l’Agence spatiale canadienne ont voyagé autour de la Lune à bord du vaisseau Orion pendant environ 10 jours. Les ingénieurs et scientifiques d’Ames ont amélioré le bouclier thermique du vaisseau, réduit les vibrations du lanceur, et développé des capteurs et logiciels pour surveiller la mission en temps réel. Ils ont aussi aidé les astronautes à observer la surface lunaire pour collecter des données scientifiques importantes.

**Impact** – Le travail d’Ames a rendu la mission Artemis II plus sûre et fiable. Par exemple, ils ont corrigé des problèmes liés au bouclier thermique qui protège les astronautes lors du retour dans l’atmosphère. Leur contribution à l’aérodynamique du lanceur a diminué les vibrations dangereuses au décollage. Les observations guidées par Ames aideront à choisir les futurs sites d’atterrissage sur la Lune. Ces progrès préparent la NASA à des missions spatiales plus longues et complexes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les données et technologies d’Artemis II soutiendront les prochaines missions Artemis, notamment le premier retour humain sur la surface lunaire. Ames continuera à tester et améliorer les systèmes spatiaux pour garantir la sécurité et le succès des futures missions. Les scientifiques étudieront aussi les nouvelles observations lunaires pour mieux planifier l’exploration.

**Phrase clé** – Le centre de recherche Ames de la NASA a joué un rôle essentiel pour faire d’Artemis II une étape sûre et réussie vers le retour des humains sur la Lune.
articles.personality_title_es: El centro Ames de la NASA tiene un papel clave en el éxito de la misión Artemis II alrededor de la Luna
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA completó recientemente Artemis II, la primera misión con astronautas alrededor de la Luna en más de 50 años. Esta misión probó nuevas tecnologías y preparó futuros alunizajes. El Centro de Investigación Ames en California ayudó a que esta misión fuera posible.

**Qué pasó** – El 1 de abril de 2026, cuatro astronautas de la NASA y la Agencia Espacial Canadiense viajaron alrededor de la Luna en la nave Orion durante unos 10 días. Los ingenieros y científicos de Ames mejoraron el escudo térmico de la nave, redujeron las vibraciones del cohete, y desarrollaron sensores y software para monitorear la misión en tiempo real. También guiaron a los astronautas para observar la superficie lunar y recopilar datos científicos importantes.

**Impacto** – El trabajo de Ames hizo que la misión Artemis II fuera más segura y confiable. Por ejemplo, solucionaron problemas con el escudo térmico que protege a los astronautas durante el regreso a la Tierra. Su trabajo en la aerodinámica del cohete redujo vibraciones peligrosas al despegar. Las observaciones dirigidas por Ames ayudarán a elegir futuros sitios de aterrizaje en la Luna. Estos avances preparan a la NASA para misiones espaciales más largas y complejas.

**Próximo paso** – Los datos y tecnologías de Artemis II apoyarán futuras misiones Artemis, incluyendo el primer regreso humano a la superficie lunar. Ames continuará probando y mejorando sistemas espaciales para asegurar que las próximas misiones sean aún más seguras y exitosas. Los científicos también estudiarán las nuevas observaciones lunares para planificar mejor la exploración.

**Frase clave** – El Centro de Investigación Ames de la NASA fue fundamental para que Artemis II fuera un paso seguro y exitoso hacia el regreso de humanos a la Luna.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a sleek spacecraft shaped like the Orion capsule gracefully orbiting a softly glowing, cratered Moon with a stylized representation of four interconnected stars symbolizing the Artemis II crew; below, subtle abstract shapes of layered heat shield textures and aerodynamic fins represent Ames Research Center’s engineering contributions, all set against a calm deep space backdrop with gentle earthrise colors on the horizon.

articles.title: NASA Invests in Small Businesses Innovating for Space and Earth
articles.summary: Continuing NASA’s longtime support of American industry, the agency announced its selection of more than 30 companies to develop innovative technology through its  Small Business Innovation Research (SBIR) and Small Business Technology Transfer (STTR) program. With these awards, NASA is investing approximately $16.3 million in seed funding of technology solutions to benefit the agency and energize the space economy.
articles.content:
Continuing NASA’s longtime support of American industry, the agency announced its selection of more than 30 companies to develop innovative technology through its Small Business Innovation Research (SBIR) and Small Business Technology Transfer (STTR) program. With these awards, NASA is investing approximately $16.3 million in seed funding of technology solutions to benefit the agency and energize the space economy.

“NASA’s support of early-stage technology, and the businesses driving these innovations, produces critical advancements for our most ambitious endeavors,” said Jason L. Kessler, program executive for NASA’s SBIR/STTR program at the agency’s headquarters in Washington. “We remain committed to fostering small businesses and research institutions that could support America’s presence on the Moon, advance human exploration of Mars, and improve the quality of life on Earth.”

The new awards come from two areas of NASA’s SBIR/STTR Program offering distinct benefits.

Focused on commercialization, the SBIR Ignite initiative gives small businesses a chance to successfully market their technology, even beyond the potential for use in NASA’s missions. The newly selected Phase I contracts – proposed by 15 firms representing 10 states across the country – will receive up to $150,000 to establish the merit and feasibility of their proposed innovation.

Farther down the development path are awardees announced for NASA’s STTR contracts, provided to small businesses partnered with research institutions, aiming to unlock the power and innovative thinking of the country’s universities and research centers. These Phase II awards, with 17 contracts valued at up to $850,000 each, target demonstration, and delivery of innovative technology.

These awardees will perform early-stage research and development in areas such as in-space manufacturing, advanced battery technologies, lunar landings, and advanced propulsion for air and spacecraft.

The projects receiving awards include:

Bio-inspired materials to help robots get a grip in space

SBIR Ignite Phase I award: Nanoscale Labs – Austin, Texas

Grasping objects in space is difficult for robots because traditional vacuum grippers fail in the vacuum of space and debris as well as spacecraft come in unique shapes. To solve this, Nanoscale Labs created a sprayable dry adhesive, inspired by geckos, that offers low-cost manufacturing, stronger adhesion, and self-cleaning resistance to space dust.

Learning to repair and replace in space

SBIR Ignite Phase I award: QuesTek Innovations LLC – Evanston, Illinois

To live and work in space for long durations, future astronauts may need to be welders, fixing and replacing parts as they explore low Earth orbit or deep space. But welding requires gravity, which presents a challenge in the space environment. As a solution, QuesTek Innovations created a simulation toolkit that will use computer modeling to predict how the properties of welded materials change in space and optimize the processes used.

Keeping a closer eye on lightning storms

STTR Phase II award: ASTER Labs, Inc. – Shoreview, Minnesota

Tracking lightning from low Earth orbit offers higher-resolution data but poses unique challenges to rapidly moving satellites due to their limited field of view. To address this, ASTER Labs developed the STORM Module, a software system that can automatically identify, track, and predict the movement of storms in real time. In partnership with the University of Alabama, Tuscaloosa, ASTER Labs will enhance and test this capability using simulated data and real lightning observations. The system will also be validated laboratory demonstrations, confirming real-time performance under realistic operating conditions. This technology aims to improve severe weather forecasting and may be adapted to track wildfires or floods.

Monitoring astronaut and earthling health with extended reality and AI

STTR Phase II award: Tietronix Software, Inc. – Houston

To support the physical and cognitive health of future astronauts, Tietronix Software and UT Austin Dell Medical School are developing a portable monitoring platform. The system uses sensors, smartphone apps, and AI to track performance and deliver therapies via an extended reality interface. Now undergoing Phase II spaceflight testing, this technology could eventually provide medical assistance to patients in remote environments on Earth.

The complete lists of selected proposals are available for this SBIR Ignite Phase I solicitation and for the STTR Phase II awards on the program’s website.

This year, NASA’s SBIR/STTR program is adopting a Broad Agency Announcement (BAA) framework to increase opportunities for small businesses while enhancing agility for the agency. The 2026-2027 BAA appendices, outlining topics and subtopics for desired technology proposals, close May 21. Interested businesses and institutions are encouraged to visit the BAA Information Hub for information on applying.

NASA’s SBIR/STTR program is part of the agency’s Space Technology Mission Directorate and is managed by NASA’s Ames Research Center in California’s Silicon Valley. To learn more about the program, visit: https://www.nasa.gov/sbir_sttr/
articles.reasoning: The article reports NASA's significant investment of $16.3 million in over 30 small businesses to develop innovative technologies with applications for space exploration and Earth benefits. This funding supports early-stage research in critical areas like in-space manufacturing, advanced propulsion, and health monitoring, which have broad implications for space missions and potentially for life on Earth. The story focuses on a clear, impactful initiative with detailed examples of technological innovation, demonstrating tangible benefits for society and the space economy.
articles.personality_title: NASA funds small businesses to create new space and Earth technologies
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA has a program that helps small businesses and research groups develop new technology. This program, called SBIR/STTR, supports early ideas that could be useful for space missions or life on Earth.

**What happened** – NASA chose over 30 small companies to receive about $16.3 million in funding. These companies will work on new technologies like special materials for space robots, tools to fix things in space, and advanced ways to track storms and monitor health. The funding is split into two parts: smaller grants to test ideas and bigger contracts to develop and demonstrate the technology.

**Impact** – This funding helps small businesses turn their creative ideas into real products. For example, one company is making a sticky material inspired by geckos to help robots grab objects in space. Another is creating software to track lightning storms from satellites. These projects could improve space missions and also help people on Earth by improving weather forecasts and health care.

**What's next step** – The companies will continue their research and testing over the next months. NASA is also inviting more small businesses to apply for future funding through a new process that starts closing applications in May 2024. This will help bring even more new ideas into space technology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA is investing millions in small businesses to develop new technologies that support space exploration and improve life on Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA finance les petites entreprises pour créer de nouvelles technologies spatiales et terrestres
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA a un programme qui aide les petites entreprises et les groupes de recherche à développer de nouvelles technologies. Ce programme, appelé SBIR/STTR, soutient les premières idées pouvant être utiles pour les missions spatiales ou la vie sur Terre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA a choisi plus de 30 petites entreprises pour recevoir environ 16,3 millions de dollars de financement. Ces entreprises vont travailler sur des technologies comme des matériaux spéciaux pour les robots spatiaux, des outils pour réparer dans l’espace, et des méthodes avancées pour suivre les orages et surveiller la santé. Le financement est divisé en deux parties : de petites subventions pour tester les idées et des contrats plus importants pour développer et démontrer la technologie.

**Impact** – Ce financement aide les petites entreprises à transformer leurs idées créatives en produits réels. Par exemple, une entreprise fabrique un matériau adhésif inspiré des geckos pour aider les robots à saisir des objets dans l’espace. Une autre crée un logiciel pour suivre les orages depuis les satellites. Ces projets pourraient améliorer les missions spatiales et aussi aider les personnes sur Terre en améliorant les prévisions météorologiques et les soins de santé.

**Étape suivante** – Les entreprises vont continuer leurs recherches et leurs tests dans les prochains mois. La NASA invite également davantage de petites entreprises à postuler pour de futurs financements via un nouveau processus dont les candidatures se terminent en mai 2024. Cela permettra d’apporter encore plus d’idées nouvelles dans la technologie spatiale.

**En une phrase** – La NASA investit des millions dans les petites entreprises pour développer de nouvelles technologies qui soutiennent l’exploration spatiale et améliorent la vie sur Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA financia pequeñas empresas para crear nuevas tecnologías espaciales y terrestres
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA tiene un programa que ayuda a pequeñas empresas y grupos de investigación a desarrollar nuevas tecnologías. Este programa, llamado SBIR/STTR, apoya ideas iniciales que pueden ser útiles para misiones espaciales o para la vida en la Tierra.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA seleccionó a más de 30 pequeñas empresas para recibir alrededor de 16.3 millones de dólares en fondos. Estas empresas trabajarán en tecnologías como materiales especiales para robots espaciales, herramientas para reparar en el espacio y formas avanzadas de rastrear tormentas y monitorear la salud. El financiamiento se divide en dos partes: pequeñas subvenciones para probar ideas y contratos más grandes para desarrollar y demostrar la tecnología.

**Impacto** – Este financiamiento ayuda a las pequeñas empresas a convertir sus ideas creativas en productos reales. Por ejemplo, una empresa está creando un material adhesivo inspirado en los geckos para ayudar a los robots a agarrar objetos en el espacio. Otra desarrolla software para seguir tormentas eléctricas desde satélites. Estos proyectos podrían mejorar las misiones espaciales y también ayudar a las personas en la Tierra al mejorar los pronósticos del clima y la atención médica.

**Próximo paso** – Las empresas continuarán su investigación y pruebas en los próximos meses. La NASA también está invitando a más pequeñas empresas a solicitar financiamiento futuro a través de un nuevo proceso que cierra solicitudes en mayo de 2024. Esto ayudará a traer aún más ideas nuevas a la tecnología espacial.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La NASA está invirtiendo millones en pequeñas empresas para desarrollar nuevas tecnologías que apoyan la exploración espacial y mejoran la vida en la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing network of small, futuristic spacecraft and robotic arms gently assembling innovative technology modules in orbit around a softly illuminated Earth and Moon, symbolizing collaboration between small businesses and NASA advancing space exploration and earthly benefits.

articles.title: A bizarre new state of matter may be hiding inside Uranus and Neptune
articles.summary: Deep inside planets like Uranus and Neptune, scientists may have uncovered a bizarre new state of matter where atoms behave in unexpected ways. Advanced simulations suggest that carbon and hydrogen, under crushing pressures and scorching temperatures, can form a strange hybrid phase—part solid, part fluid—where hydrogen atoms spiral through a rigid carbon framework. This unusual “superionic” structure could reshape how heat and electricity flow inside these distant worlds, potentially helping explain their mysterious magnetic fields.
articles.content:
The deep interiors of ice giant planets such as Uranus and Neptune may contain a previously unknown form of matter. This possibility comes from new computer simulations conducted by Carnegie scientists Cong Liu and Ronald Cohen.

Their study, published in Nature Communications, suggests that carbon hydride could take on an unusual quasi-one-dimensional superionic state under the intense pressures and temperatures found far beneath the surfaces of these distant planets.

Why Planetary Interiors Matter

More than 6,000 exoplanets have been discovered so far, and that number continues to grow. To better understand how planets form and evolve, researchers from astronomy, planetary science, and Earth science are increasingly working together. By combining observations, experiments, and theoretical models, they aim to uncover the physical processes that shape planets, including how magnetic fields are generated.

This growing interest also extends to the hidden layers within planets and moons in our own Solar System. Studying what happens deep below the surface can provide clues about planetary behavior and even help scientists assess whether distant worlds could support life.

"Hot Ice" Layers Inside Ice Giants

Data on the densities of Uranus and Neptune indicate that these planets contain unusual internal layers often described as "hot ices." These regions sit beneath outer atmospheres of hydrogen and helium and above solid cores.

Scientists believe these layers are made up of water (H 2 O), methane (CH 4 ), and ammonia (NH 4 ). However, the extreme conditions in these environments likely force these familiar compounds into exotic and unfamiliar forms.

Simulating Extreme Planetary Conditions

The intense pressures and temperatures inside ice giants can produce states of matter that do not exist on Earth. To explore this, Liu and Cohen used high-performance computing and machine-learning tools to run detailed quantum simulations of carbon hydride (CH).

They modeled conditions ranging from nearly 5 million to nearly 30 million times Earth's atmospheric pressure (500 to 3,000 gigapascals) and temperatures between 6,740 and 10,340 degrees Fahrenheit (4,000 to 6,000 Kelvin).

A Strange "Spiral" Superionic State

The simulations revealed a striking structure. Carbon atoms form an ordered hexagonal framework, while hydrogen atoms move through it along spiral-like paths. This creates a quasi-one-dimensional superionic state.

Superionic materials are unusual because they behave partly like solids and partly like liquids. One type of atom remains locked in place within a crystal structure, while another type moves freely through it.

"This newly predicted carbon-hydrogen phase is particularly striking because the atomic motion is not fully three-dimensional," Cohen explained. "Instead, hydrogen moves preferentially along well-defined helical pathways embedded within an ordered carbon structure."

Implications for Heat, Electricity, and Magnetic Fields

The directional movement of hydrogen atoms could have major effects on how energy flows inside planets. It may influence how heat and electricity are transported through these deep layers.

These properties are especially important for understanding how Uranus and Neptune generate their magnetic fields, which differ in unusual ways from those of other planets.

Broader Impact Beyond Planetary Science

The findings also highlight how simple elements can behave in surprisingly complex ways under extreme conditions. Even basic compounds like carbon and hydrogen can form highly organized and unexpected structures.

"Carbon and hydrogen are among the most abundant elements in planetary materials, yet their combined behavior at giant-planet conditions remains far from fully understood," Liu concluded.

Beyond helping scientists understand distant planets, this research could also inform advances in materials science and engineering by revealing new types of directional behavior in matter.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a new state of matter inside Uranus and Neptune, which enhances understanding of planetary interiors and magnetic fields. This breakthrough has broad implications for planetary science and materials science, representing a meaningful advancement with potential wide-ranging benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover a new state of matter inside Uranus and Neptune
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been studying the deep interiors of the ice giant planets Uranus and Neptune to understand what happens under extreme pressures and temperatures. These planets have unusual layers called "hot ices," made from water, methane, and ammonia, but the exact forms of matter inside are still a mystery.

**What happened** – Researchers Cong Liu and Ronald Cohen used powerful computer simulations to study carbon and hydrogen under the intense conditions found deep inside Uranus and Neptune. They found a new state of matter called a "superionic" phase, where carbon atoms create a solid framework and hydrogen atoms move in spiral paths through it, behaving like both a solid and a liquid at the same time.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it helps explain how heat and electricity might move inside these planets. It could also clarify why Uranus and Neptune have unusual magnetic fields that are different from other planets. The finding shows that even simple elements like carbon and hydrogen can form complex and surprising structures under extreme conditions.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue to study this new state of matter to better understand planetary interiors and magnetic fields. This research might also inspire new materials with special properties for technology and engineering.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New computer simulations reveal a unique superionic state of carbon and hydrogen inside Uranus and Neptune, shedding light on their mysterious interiors and magnetic fields.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent un nouvel état de la matière à l'intérieur d'Uranus et Neptune
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient l'intérieur profond des planètes géantes glacées Uranus et Neptune pour comprendre ce qui se passe sous des pressions et des températures extrêmes. Ces planètes possèdent des couches inhabituelles appelées « glaces chaudes », composées d'eau, de méthane et d'ammoniac, mais la forme exacte de la matière à l'intérieur reste inconnue.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Les chercheurs Cong Liu et Ronald Cohen ont utilisé des simulations informatiques puissantes pour étudier le carbone et l'hydrogène dans les conditions intenses présentes profondément à l'intérieur d'Uranus et Neptune. Ils ont découvert un nouvel état de la matière appelé phase « superionique », où les atomes de carbone forment une structure solide et les atomes d'hydrogène se déplacent en spirale à travers elle, se comportant à la fois comme un solide et un liquide.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle aide à expliquer comment la chaleur et l'électricité pourraient circuler à l'intérieur de ces planètes. Elle pourrait aussi clarifier pourquoi Uranus et Neptune ont des champs magnétiques inhabituels, différents de ceux des autres planètes. Cette trouvaille montre que même des éléments simples comme le carbone et l'hydrogène peuvent former des structures complexes et surprenantes dans des conditions extrêmes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier ce nouvel état de la matière pour mieux comprendre l'intérieur des planètes et leurs champs magnétiques. Cette recherche pourrait aussi inspirer de nouveaux matériaux avec des propriétés spéciales pour la technologie et l'ingénierie.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – De nouvelles simulations informatiques révèlent un état superionique unique du carbone et de l'hydrogène à l'intérieur d'Uranus et Neptune, éclairant leurs intérieurs mystérieux et leurs champs magnétiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren un nuevo estado de la materia dentro de Urano y Neptuno
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estado estudiando el interior profundo de los planetas gigantes de hielo Urano y Neptuno para entender qué ocurre bajo presiones y temperaturas extremas. Estos planetas tienen capas inusuales llamadas "hielos calientes", formadas por agua, metano y amoníaco, pero la forma exacta de la materia en su interior sigue siendo un misterio.

**Qué pasó** – Los investigadores Cong Liu y Ronald Cohen usaron potentes simulaciones por computadora para estudiar el carbono y el hidrógeno bajo las intensas condiciones que existen en el interior de Urano y Neptuno. Encontraron un nuevo estado de la materia llamado fase "superiónica", donde los átomos de carbono forman una estructura sólida y los de hidrógeno se mueven en caminos en espiral a través de ella, comportándose como sólido y líquido al mismo tiempo.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque ayuda a explicar cómo se mueve el calor y la electricidad dentro de estos planetas. También podría aclarar por qué Urano y Neptuno tienen campos magnéticos inusuales, diferentes a los de otros planetas. El hallazgo muestra que incluso elementos simples como el carbono y el hidrógeno pueden formar estructuras complejas y sorprendentes bajo condiciones extremas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando este nuevo estado de la materia para entender mejor los interiores planetarios y sus campos magnéticos. Esta investigación también podría inspirar nuevos materiales con propiedades especiales para la tecnología y la ingeniería.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Nuevas simulaciones por computadora revelan un estado superiónico único de carbono e hidrógeno dentro de Urano y Neptuno, arrojando luz sobre sus misteriosos interiores y campos magnéticos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting depicting a glowing, hexagonal lattice structure symbolizing carbon atoms, with delicate spiral pathways of flowing light weaving through it to represent hydrogen atoms moving in a quasi-one-dimensional superionic state, set against an abstract, swirling backdrop evoking the deep, hot ice layers inside Uranus and Neptune.

articles.title: Curiosity Blog, Sols 4867-4872: Sand Fill In Antofagasta Crater and Finding Our Next Drill Target
articles.summary: Written by Lucy Lim, Planetary Scientist at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center Earth planning date: Friday, April 17, 2026 At the beginning of the week, Curiosity arrived right on target on the rim of the 10-meter (33 feet) “Antofagasta” crater.   The crater looked fresh and deep as we had hoped with a nice well-defined […]
articles.content:
NASA’s Mars rover Curiosity acquired this image using its Right Navigation Camera on April 13, 2026 — Sol 4865, or Martian day 4,865 of the Mars Science Laboratory mission, at 21:36:04 UTC. NASA/JPL-Caltech

Written by Lucy Lim, Planetary Scientist at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center

Earth planning date: Friday, April 17, 2026

At the beginning of the week, Curiosity arrived right on target on the rim of the 10-meter (33 feet) “Antofagasta” crater.

The crater looked fresh and deep as we had hoped with a nice well-defined rim that didn't look too eroded, but the bottom of it turned out to be filled with dark rippled sandy material that covered up the most interesting rock layers. There were a few rock exposures just above the sand cover that seemed like they might have been deep enough to have been sheltered from space radiation between the time their sediments were deposited and the crater-forming impact, but reaching them from the rim would have put the rover at such an awkward angle that we wouldn't have been able to deliver the sample to the instruments. It's possible that we might have been able to get into a better position by instead placing the rover on the rippled crater fill, but the chance that the rover could get stuck in all that sand made it much too high a risk. We also looked at the nearby blocks in case they could have been ejecta from the crater, but since all the rocks visible in the crater wall looked very similar to each other, there wasn't a good way to tell which ejecta blocks might have come from the deeper layers of the crater. Because of this, the team decided against attempting to drill in or around the crater.

Luckily the rover's workspace was rich with interesting bedrock targets including polygonal features. We planned detailed imaging of the crater and nearby buttes together with APXS geochemistry, MAHLI close-up imaging and ChemCam LIBS geochemistry of the polygon-bearing rocks on the crater rim. The plan was rounded out with our ongoing observations of the present-day Martian environment, including monitoring for dust-devil activity and regular measurements of atmospheric opacity and clouds.

Meanwhile, with the decision not to drill at Antofagasta, we started planning our next drill campaign! To plan our drill strategy in this post-boxwork section of the layered sulfate strata, we've been looking at the exposed layering in the buttes above us as we have been driving up through “Valle Grande.” Based on these observations, team members have mapped out a succession of varying depositional styles and levels of diagenetic activity. As we climb southwards, the rover will reach these rock layers one by one.

It's been quite a while since we've drilled into the layered sulfate rocks outside the distinctive regions of the boxwork-forming unit and Gediz Vallis. The last "typical" layered sulfate drill was the "Mineral King" campaign in February/March 2024, more than 150 meters (492 feet) lower in elevation. So for our next drill campaign our goal is to measure a representative bedrock sample from the layers just above the boxworks. The Sol 4870 workspace turned out to have a drillable-looking, representative-looking block right in front of the rover so we have planned our preliminary APXS, MAHLI, and ChemCam geochemistry on the potential drill target, “Atacama,” in addition to some measurements on surrounding blocks for context. If the results look good we'll proceed with the preload test in the next plan and look forward to a new set of drill data on Mars.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant ongoing Mars exploration mission by NASA's Curiosity rover, detailing its successful arrival at a crater, scientific observations, and planning of a new drill campaign to study Martian geology. This represents a meaningful scientific achievement with broad public interest and potential to advance knowledge about Mars and planetary science.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Curiosity rover explores new crater and plans next Mars drill mission
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Curiosity rover has been exploring Mars for many years, studying rock layers to understand the planet’s history. Recently, it reached the rim of a small crater called Antofagasta to investigate the area.

**What happened** – In April 2026, Curiosity arrived exactly at the edge of the 10-meter-wide Antofagasta crater. The crater looked fresh and well-defined, but its bottom was filled with dark, sandy ripples that covered the rocks scientists wanted to study. Because the sand was risky to drive on and the exposed rocks were hard to reach safely, the team decided not to drill there. Instead, they focused on nearby rock formations with interesting patterns and planned close-up imaging and chemical tests.

**Impact** – This careful decision shows how NASA’s team balances exploration with safety to get the best scientific results. Even without drilling in the crater, the rover found valuable targets nearby that can tell us about Mars’ past. The detailed study of layered rocks helps scientists learn how Mars’ environment changed over time.

**What's next step** – The rover’s team started planning a new drilling campaign at a nearby site called Atacama, where the rocks look safe and promising for sampling. If initial tests go well, Curiosity will drill into these layered sulfate rocks to collect fresh samples. This will add important information about Mars’ geology and the processes that shaped its surface.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Curiosity’s careful exploration near Antofagasta crater leads to a new drill target, continuing NASA’s quest to uncover Mars’ geological history.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le rover Curiosity de la NASA explore un nouveau cratère et prépare sa prochaine mission de forage sur Mars
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le rover Curiosity de la NASA explore Mars depuis plusieurs années pour étudier les couches rocheuses et comprendre l’histoire de la planète. Récemment, il a atteint le bord d’un petit cratère appelé Antofagasta pour l’examiner.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En avril 2026, Curiosity est arrivé exactement au bord du cratère Antofagasta, large de 10 mètres. Le cratère semblait récent et bien défini, mais son fond était rempli de sable sombre et ondulé qui cachait les roches que les scientifiques voulaient étudier. Comme le sable était risqué pour se déplacer et que les roches exposées étaient difficiles d’accès, l’équipe a décidé de ne pas forer là. Ils ont plutôt étudié des formations rocheuses voisines avec des motifs intéressants, en prévoyant des images détaillées et des analyses chimiques.

**Impact** – Cette décision prudente montre comment l’équipe de la NASA équilibre exploration et sécurité pour obtenir les meilleurs résultats scientifiques. Même sans forage dans le cratère, le rover a trouvé des cibles précieuses à proximité qui peuvent révéler le passé de Mars. L’étude détaillée des couches rocheuses aide les scientifiques à comprendre l’évolution de l’environnement martien.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prépare une nouvelle campagne de forage sur un site proche appelé Atacama, où les roches semblent sûres et prometteuses pour des échantillons. Si les premiers tests sont concluants, Curiosity forera ces roches sulfatiques stratifiées pour collecter des échantillons frais. Cela apportera des informations importantes sur la géologie de Mars et les processus qui ont façonné sa surface.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’exploration prudente de Curiosity près du cratère Antofagasta ouvre la voie à une nouvelle cible de forage, poursuivant la quête de la NASA pour comprendre l’histoire géologique de Mars.
articles.personality_title_es: El rover Curiosity de la NASA explora un nuevo cráter y planifica su próxima misión de perforación en Marte
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El rover Curiosity de la NASA ha estado explorando Marte durante muchos años, estudiando las capas de roca para entender la historia del planeta. Recientemente, llegó al borde de un pequeño cráter llamado Antofagasta para investigarlo.

**Qué pasó** – En abril de 2026, Curiosity llegó justo al borde del cráter Antofagasta, que mide 10 metros de ancho. El cráter parecía fresco y bien definido, pero el fondo estaba lleno de arena oscura con ondas que cubrían las rocas que los científicos querían estudiar. Debido al riesgo de que el rover se quedara atascado en la arena y a que las rocas expuestas eran difíciles de alcanzar, el equipo decidió no perforar allí. En cambio, se concentraron en formaciones rocosas cercanas con patrones interesantes y planearon imágenes detalladas y pruebas químicas.

**Impacto** – Esta decisión cuidadosa muestra cómo el equipo de la NASA equilibra la exploración con la seguridad para obtener los mejores resultados científicos. Aunque no perforaron en el cráter, el rover encontró objetivos valiosos cerca que pueden contar más sobre el pasado de Marte. El estudio detallado de las capas rocosas ayuda a los científicos a aprender cómo cambió el ambiente marciano.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo comenzó a planear una nueva campaña de perforación en un sitio cercano llamado Atacama, donde las rocas parecen seguras y prometedoras para tomar muestras. Si las pruebas iniciales son buenas, Curiosity perforará estas rocas de sulfato en capas para recoger muestras frescas. Esto aportará información importante sobre la geología de Marte y los procesos que moldearon su superficie.

**Resumen en una frase** – La exploración cuidadosa de Curiosity cerca del cráter Antofagasta lleva a una nueva meta de perforación, continuando la misión de la NASA para descubrir la historia geológica de Marte.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a rugged Martian landscape featuring the sharp rim of a small, sandy-filled crater with rippled dark sand inside, surrounded by layered rocky buttes with polygonal patterns, under a soft reddish sky; a stylized robotic rover silhouette stands near a prominent drillable rock block labeled by its unique texture, symbolizing exploration and careful scientific study on Mars.
